message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 16
Recent changes: Green phase added loyalty discount. Added Customer interface, LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS=2, LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE=0.2. quotePremium now takes (step, customer) and computes policyBase + itemSurcharges - loyaltyDiscount + firstInsurance + fee (loyalty = 20% of policyBase when yearsWithMHPCO >= 2). runScenario threads scenario.customer.

Refactor while keeping all tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design, compute APP mass before/after. quotePremium is accumulating several policy-wide percentage modifiers (first-insurance +10%, loyalty -20%) plus item surcharges. Consider whether the premium formula reads clearly. Upcoming tests add follow-up contract discount (-15% on contracts after the first) which will need scenario/step-index context — do NOT implement that ahead. Make only current-justified improvements.

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Let me run the tests first to confirm the current state is green.

content.text:
All 16 tests pass. Now let me analyze the code.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Looking at `quotePremium`: the name describes what it does (computes a quote premium). It's accurate. Other names are clear: `policyBasePremium`, `itemSurcharges`, `surchargeForItem`, `typeBasePremium`, `isComponent`, `countByType`. All reveal intent well.

The Green phase prompt highlights the concern: `quotePremium` accumulates several **policy-wide percentage modifiers** (first-insurance +10%, loyalty -20%) plus item surcharges. The formula in the return statement mixes base, surcharges, percentage modifiers, and a flat fee in one expression. Let me look at whether intent reads clearly there.

## Step 2: APP Mass — current `quotePremium`

```
const quotePremium = (step, customer) => {
  const policyBase = policyBasePremium(step.items);          // binding(1) + invocation(2) = 3
  const firstInsurance = policyBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;  // binding(1) + bind(1)+bind(1) + mult-invocation(2) = 5  [policyBase, FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE]
  const loyaltyDiscount = cond ? policyBase*RATE : 0;        // binding(1) + conditional(4) + bind+bind(2)+mult(2) + const(1) + (>= invocation 2)+bind+bind(2) = 14
  return Math.ceil(policyBase + surcharges + ... + fee);     // Math.ceil(2) + itemSurcharges(2) + 4 bindings + PROCESSING_FEE(1) + 4 arithmetic ops(8) = 17
}
```

The conditional `loyaltyDiscount` and the mixed final expression are the dense parts.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** Green now.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** This is where I see the strongest improvement. There are two policy-wide percentage modifiers computed in different styles: `firstInsurance` is computed inline as a positive add, `loyaltyDiscount` as a conditional subtraction, and they're scattered through a single dense return expression. The premium is conceptually: `base + itemSurcharges + policyModifiers + fee`. Extracting the percentage modifiers into a named helper would reveal that structure and unify the two modifiers' representation.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** `firstInsurance` and `loyaltyDiscount` both follow the shape `policyBase * rate`. There's conceptual duplication in "a policy-wide percentage of the base." 

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** No dead code.

Let me extract a `policyModifiers` helper that computes the net policy-wide percentage adjustment, and a small `rateAdjustment` helper to remove the shape duplication. This reveals the premium formula as `base + surcharges + modifiers + fee`.

I'll make one focused change: extract the policy-wide modifier computation.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_06-28-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

✓ src/claim-office.spec.ts  (36 tests | 20 skipped) 4ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  16 passed | 20 todo (36)
   Start at  06:52:31
   Duration  158ms (transform 32ms, setup 0ms, collect 30ms, tests 4ms, environment 0ms, prepare 42ms)

content.text:
All 16 tests still green.

## Step 5: APP Mass — refactored `quotePremium`

```
const isLoyal = (customer) => customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS;
  // bind(1) + invocation>=(2) + bind(1) = 4

const policyModifierRate = (customer) =>
  FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE - (isLoyal(customer) ? LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0);
  // bind(1) + subtract(2) + isLoyal-invocation(2) + conditional(4) + bind(1) + const(1) = 11

const quotePremium = (step, customer) => {
  const policyBase = policyBasePremium(step.items);          // bind(1)+inv(2) = 3
  const policyModifiers = policyBase * policyModifierRate(customer); // bind(1)+bind(1)+mult(2)+inv(2) = 6
  return Math.ceil(policyBase + itemSurcharges + policyModifiers + PROCESSING_FEE);
  // Math.ceil(2)+itemSurcharges(2)+3 bindings+const(1)+3 adds(6) = 14
}
```

`quotePremium` body alone dropped from ~36 to ~23. The conditional and one percentage-multiplication moved out of the hot function into the small, single-purpose `policyModifierRate`. Total mass across the three new functions (~35) is roughly flat versus the original (~39), but it's now distributed into named, single-responsibility pieces.

## Step 6: Decision

**Refactoring Applied (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent, with Rule 3 support):**

- The two policy-wide percentage modifiers (first-insurance +10%, loyalty −20%) were computed in two different styles and scattered through one dense return expression. They are conceptually the same thing: a percentage of the policy base. I unified them into a single net `policyModifierRate(customer)` and applied it once as `policyBase * policyModifierRate(customer)`.
- The premium formula now reads as the clear four-term sum it actually is: `policyBase + itemSurcharges + policyModifiers + PROCESSING_FEE`.
- Extracted `isLoyal(customer)` as a named predicate, removing the inline threshold comparison from the arithmetic.
- This removed the conceptual duplication of `policyBase * <rate>` appearing twice (Rule 3).

I deliberately did **not** introduce any scenario/step-index plumbing for the upcoming follow-up-contract discount — `policyModifierRate` takes only `customer` today, exactly what current tests justify.

## Step 7: Report

```
Refactoring Complete:
Refactoring: Extracted isLoyal() predicate and policyModifierRate() to unify
             the two policy-wide percentage modifiers; quotePremium return now
             reads as base + surcharges + modifiers + fee.
Mass Change: quotePremium body ~36 → ~23 (modifiers extracted into named helpers;
             total ~flat, better distributed). Conditional moved out of the main formula.
Tests: All 16 passing.
```

Files relevant to this task:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_06-28-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_06-28-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`

The refactor unified the first-insurance and loyalty modifiers into a single `policyModifierRate(customer)` helper plus an `isLoyal(customer)` predicate, so the premium formula reads as a clean four-term sum. All 16 tests remain green. No look-ahead implementation was added for the upcoming follow-up-contract discount.
